metric_value: ~$6 billion in hardware revenue; NT application revenues ~$2 billion
notes: Aberdeen estimates for FY1997.

metric_name: NT server hardware market growth forecast
metric_value: 50% annual growth for next 4 years; hardware market to grow to $9 billion by 2000
notes: Total market includes slowdown in 1998-1999 due to Y2K resource diversion and inventory issues.

metric_value: NT application revenues ~$2B in 1997; will grow to ~$13 billion by 2001
notes: Phenomenal projected growth; will tax IS community.

metric_value: Global Unix application server market $19.6 billion in 1996; projected $40 billion in 2000; 4.5x larger than NT market
notes: Unix revenues project to $40B in 2000 vs. NT $9B — NT remains secondary.

metric_value: Windows NT servers part of strategic plans for more than 90% of Fortune 1000 IS executives
notes: By end of century NT will factor in most IS and LOB acquisition plans.

metric_value: NT deployments increasingly require professional services; IS without staff NT experience common
notes: Three deployment paths: internal IS / OEM pro services / SI and resellers.

metric_value: Windows NT 5.0 to be released sometime in 1999; will add Active Directory
notes: expert-opinion

metric_value: Windows 2000 shipped February 17 2000 — verify and update.
notes: Windows 2000 shipped February 17 2000 — verify and update.

metric_value: Intel IA-64 (Merced) arriving late 1999; IA-32 to IA-64 conversion likely takes 5+ years
notes: Pentium Pro and Pentium II Xeon bridge period.

metric_value: Itanium shipped June 2001; verify market adoption pace.
notes: Itanium shipped June 2001; verify market adoption pace.

metric_name: Digital Alpha market share and roadmap
metric_value: Alpha holds ~2% of NT server market; reaching 660 MHz by H2 1998; projected 1 GHz by 2000; strong for transaction workloads
notes: FX32 technology enables Intel x86 binary recompilation for Alpha.

metric_value: MSCS (Wolfpack) introduced fall 1997; two-node failover only; not full HA; no automatic load balancing
notes: Very much a work in progress; more advanced clustering needed for enterprise.

metric_value: Suppliers divided: proprietary >4-way now vs. waiting for Intel standard 8-way chipset; few NT apps exploit multi-threading
notes: IS executives consolidating small NT servers into larger SMP platforms.

metric_name: NT operating system shipment growth forecast
metric_value: NT Server OS shipments to increase 88% in 1998; over 100% in 1999
notes: OS growth faster than hardware because of new version releases.

metric_value: Compaq largest NT/Intel server supplier; ProLiant from entry-level to 4-way and 8-way; direct and indirect model transition
notes: First >2-way NT cluster with documented performance (6-node Oracle Parallel Server with ServerNet).

metric_value: Dell company to watch in 1998; transitioning from SOHO/SME to enterprise; 8-way rack servers expected by year-end
notes: Direct model and JIT manufacturing key advantage; first supplier to disavow uni-processor servers.

metric_value: IBM Netfinity (rebranded PC Server) in downward spiral; early 1998 Intel server revenues declining; technology leadership lacking
notes: IBM advantage: Global Services and Lotus packaging; but less focused on cutting-edge server tech.

metric_value: HP NetServer line strong in SMP and systems management; 8-way and 16-way architectures in development; co-developing IA-64 with Intel
notes: HP and IBM neck-and-neck in NT revenues in 1997.

metric_value: Digital has largest NT installed base of clustered systems; >1600 certified NT professionals; being acquired by Compaq
notes: Alpha fabrication sold to Intel; FX32 included in NT 5.0.

metric_value: NT remains less robust/scalable/enterprise-ready than mature Unix platforms; second choice for larger mission-critical apps
notes: Change expected over next 2 years as Microsoft and Intel introduce new products.

metric_value: NT essentially supports one application per OS copy; keeps NT servers on periphery of larger database environments
notes: Multi-application support expected in future NT versions.

metric_value: Misconception that NT/Intel servers are a Y2K safe harbor is false; require software updates at application/OS/hardware levels
notes: 386/486 processor systems require manual rollover due to legacy BIOS; Windows 95 has file-date bugs.

metric_value: Intel microprocessors in >95% of NT servers; IA-32 (Pentium II Xeon for >4-way) and IA-64 (late 1999) roadmap
notes: Intel gives image of non-competitor but drives standards through alliance partners.
